deckDescription: Architectural patterns for building scalable distributed systems. Each card represents a design decision, not a specific tool.

cards.name: Event-driven Runtime
cards.description: Non-blocking I/O loop handles thousands of concurrent connections on a single thread. Examples: Node.js, Deno, Go.
cards.bestFor: Real-time apps, WebSocket connections, high-concurrency APIs with many simultaneous clients
cards.notGreatFor: CPU-heavy processing or ML inference — a thread-per-request model handles blocking work better

synergies.reason: Native async document access, no ORM overhead

cards.name: Thread-per-request Backend
cards.description: Synchronous request handling on a thread pool. Each request blocks until complete. Examples: Flask, Django, Rails, Spring.
cards.bestFor: CPU-bound tasks, ML model inference, data processing pipelines, teams with Python/Ruby/JVM expertise
cards.notGreatFor: High-concurrency real-time connections — threads are expensive at 10K+ concurrent users

synergies.reason: Native client libraries for batch query optimization

cards.name: Reverse Proxy
cards.description: Sits in front of backend servers, handles SSL termination, static file serving, and connection pooling. Examples: Nginx, HAProxy, Caddy.
cards.bestFor: Offloading SSL/TLS, serving static files at the edge, connection pooling for backend servers
cards.notGreatFor: Dynamic traffic distribution across many instances — use a dedicated load balancer instead

synergies.reason: Edge caching for static assets + origin caching layers
Both handle SSL termination, routing, and request transformation — stacking them adds an extra network hop with no benefit

cards.name: Relational Database
cards.description: Row-oriented storage with ACID transactions, foreign keys, and a rich query language. Examples: PostgreSQL, MySQL, SQLite.
cards.bestFor: Structured data with relationships, financial transactions, systems where data consistency is non-negotiable
cards.notGreatFor: Rapidly evolving schemas or document-shaped data — migrations slow you down

cards.name: Document Store
cards.description: Schema-flexible JSON/BSON document storage. No migrations required. Examples: MongoDB, DynamoDB, Firestore.
cards.bestFor: Flexible or hierarchical data (user profiles, product catalogs), teams iterating quickly on data shape
cards.notGreatFor: Complex relational queries or systems that need strong ACID guarantees across multiple entities

cards.name: In-memory Cache
cards.description: Sub-millisecond key-value store in RAM. Also supports pub/sub, sorted sets, and Lua scripting. Examples: Redis, KeyDB.
cards.bestFor: Session storage, hot query caching, rate limiting state, pub/sub messaging — anything needing microsecond reads
cards.notGreatFor: Large datasets that exceed available RAM — use a distributed cache with consistent hashing instead

synergies.reason: Caching query results eliminates repeated DB round-trips
Async pipelining multiplies cache throughput

cards.name: Distributed Object Cache
cards.description: Horizontally sharded key-value cache designed for simple, high-throughput object caching across many nodes. Examples: Memcached.
cards.bestFor: Simple object caching when you need to scale cache capacity linearly across many servers
cards.notGreatFor: Rich data structures, persistence, or pub/sub — use an in-memory cache for those features

synergies.reason: Two competing cache layers cause invalidation storms and stale data

cards.name: Containerization
cards.description: Package application and dependencies into a portable image. Guarantees identical behaviour across dev, staging, and prod. Examples: Docker, Podman.
cards.bestFor: Reproducible deployments, eliminating environment drift, foundation for container orchestration
cards.notGreatFor: Teams not ready for DevOps tooling overhead — start without it if the team is small

cards.name: Container Orchestrator
cards.description: Automates scheduling, scaling, self-healing, and rolling deployments of containerized workloads. Examples: Kubernetes, Nomad.
cards.bestFor: Running many services at scale, automated failover, horizontal pod autoscaling, zero-downtime deploys
cards.notGreatFor: Small teams or simple apps — the operational overhead is significant

synergies.reason: Self-healing: failed containers restart automatically
Horizontal pod autoscaling under load
Over-provisioned thread pools waste orchestrator resources at scale

cards.name: Load Balancer
cards.description: Distributes incoming requests across a pool of backend instances using round-robin, least-connections, or weighted algorithms.
cards.bestFor: Horizontal scaling, eliminating single points of failure, health-check-based traffic routing
cards.notGreatFor: Stateful connections (WebSockets) without sticky sessions — use a message queue for async workloads instead

synergies.reason: Health-check probes + graceful connection draining

cards.name: Content Delivery Network
cards.description: Serves static assets (JS, CSS, images, fonts) from edge PoPs close to each user. Also caches API responses at the edge.
cards.bestFor: Reducing latency for geographically distributed users, offloading static file serving from your origin
cards.notGreatFor: Dynamic, personalised API responses that can't be cached — those still hit your origin

synergies.reason: Object storage as CDN origin with automatic edge caching

cards.name: Observability Stack
cards.description: Metrics, logs, and traces in one place. Dashboards show what changed, when, and why. Examples: Prometheus + Grafana, Datadog.
cards.bestFor: Understanding system behaviour in production, setting SLO alerts, debugging incidents faster
cards.notGreatFor: Pre-production — invest here once you have real traffic to observe

synergies.reason: Pod health metrics feed auto-recovery triggers

cards.name: Rate Limiter
cards.description: Throttles requests per client (IP, API key, user). Prevents abuse, protects backend capacity, and enforces fair usage.
cards.bestFor: Public APIs, protecting expensive endpoints, preventing one bad actor from starving other users
cards.notGreatFor: Internal-only services — the latency overhead is unnecessary inside a trusted network

synergies.reason: Centralised rate limiting at the gateway layer
Rate limiter throttles requests before the queue can absorb bursts

cards.name: Message Queue
cards.description: Decouples producers from consumers via a persistent buffer. Workers pull jobs at their own pace. Examples: RabbitMQ, SQS, Kafka.
cards.bestFor: Async workloads, absorbing traffic bursts, background jobs, guaranteeing at-least-once delivery
cards.notGreatFor: Request-response flows where the client needs an immediate answer — adds latency

synergies.reason: Cache-backed job deduplication and acknowledgement

cards.name: Object Storage
cards.description: Infinitely scalable blob/file storage accessible via HTTP. Pay per GB stored and per request. Examples: S3, GCS, Azure Blob.
cards.bestFor: User uploads, static assets, database backups, audit logs — anything files or blobs
cards.notGreatFor: Low-latency random reads on structured data — use a database for that

cards.name: Search & Analytics Engine
cards.description: Inverted-index store built for full-text search, faceted filtering, and real-time aggregations. Examples: Elasticsearch, OpenSearch.
cards.bestFor: Full-text search, log analytics, autocomplete, faceted product search
cards.notGreatFor: Primary transactional data store — it sacrifices ACID for search performance

synergies.reason: Offloads search queries from the primary DB

cards.name: API Gateway
cards.description: Single entry point that handles routing, auth verification, request transformation, and throttling for all services.
cards.bestFor: Microservices architectures, centralising cross-cutting concerns (auth, logging, CORS) away from individual services
cards.notGreatFor: Monoliths or simple single-service apps — adds latency with no benefit

synergies.reason: Gateway routing + load balancer distribution
Both handle SSL termination, routing, and request transformation — stacking them adds an extra network hop with no benefit

cards.name: Web Application Firewall
cards.description: Layer-7 traffic inspection that blocks SQLi, XSS, and volumetric attack traffic before it reaches your origin.
cards.bestFor: Public-facing APIs, regulatory compliance (PCI-DSS, HIPAA), protecting against OWASP Top 10
cards.notGreatFor: Internal services — the cost and latency aren't worth it for traffic that never touches the internet

synergies.reason: Layered defence: WAF blocks known attacks, rate limiter handles unknown abuse

cards.name: Auto Scaler
cards.description: Watches CPU, memory, or custom metrics and adds/removes compute instances automatically to match demand.
cards.bestFor: Bursty traffic with predictable patterns, cost efficiency at scale — pay only for what you need
cards.notGreatFor: Apps with slow startup times — instances take too long to come online during sudden spikes

synergies.reason: HPA + cluster autoscaler act at both pod and node level
Load balancer automatically routes to newly scaled instances

cards.name: CI/CD Pipeline
cards.description: Automated build → test → deploy pipeline triggered on every commit. Catches regressions before they reach production.
cards.bestFor: Any team deploying more than once a week — automation eliminates human error and speeds up feedback loops
cards.notGreatFor: Pre-MVP stage where deployment is rare and the cost of setup exceeds the benefit

synergies.reason: Reproducible container builds mean consistent deployments
Deploy health checks + automatic rollback on error spike
